You are the DIRTY-ROOM integrator for the tamarin-rs relicensing campaign, working in /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs. Read /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md (integration rules) and the relevant sections of /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/INTEGRATION_REPORT.md first — this round's clean-room work aimed to close exactly the blockers documented there.
RULES: adapters and extraction only — NEVER transplant ported logic into clean files (a clean file acquiring a GPL header from scripts/gen_license_headers.py is a provenance violation: stop and report it). Vendored clean sources get mechanical fixes only (crate:: path adjustments, include paths); they stay headerless. If a gap still blocks a swap, KEEP the ported path with its header and report the gap precisely — never force it. Code comments describe current code only. Do not run git commits. Return a plain-text summary; append a new dated section to INTEGRATION_REPORT.md with per-unit deleted/kept/header-delta detail.
VALIDATION (at the end): cargo build --workspace; cargo test -p tamarin-parser -p tamarin-theory -p tamarin-prover -p tamarin-server; the wellformedness fixture suite; scripts/gen_license_headers.py followed by --check (expect 0 stale).

Integrate the round-4 clean-room closures for units C, D, E, G. Similarity audits PASSED for: C, D, E, G. FAILED (do NOT integrate these — keep-and-report): (none).
For each passed unit, re-sync the clean sources from the cluster workspace into the repo (vendoring recipes are in INTEGRATION_REPORT.md; the wf/ re-sync MUST preserve crates/tamarin-parser/src/wf/order.rs and its two mod-lines), then perform the swap the prior blockers prevented:
- C: route the formula-terms check through the clean sort-aware binding, and the run.rs/theory_io.rs guardedness call sites through the clean semantic guardedness. Gate: parser suite + wellformedness fixture suite + the captured node-binder Free-x case, all byte-green. When green, DELETE the ported check_terms module and elaborate::check_guarded_wf (locations in INTEGRATION_REPORT.md section C.2) plus any dead code they leave.
- D: route argument parsing through the clean typed parse (a thin mapping to the existing Args struct is fine; the clean parser now accepts the Rust-only flags) and batch output framing through the clean stream-aware framing. Gate: cli_e2e + captured CLI fixtures byte-green including the stdout/stderr split and the output-path column. When green, DELETE the ported parse_args/validation bodies and the ported framing in run.rs.
- E: re-attempt the expand rewire. The three previously-failing captured tests (bare nullary macro; AccLemma; CaseTest non-expansion) must pass UNMODIFIED; the positional-zip macros:-preservation adapter from last round is allowed. When green, DELETE the ported expand_theory_macros/expand_items/expand_rule.
- G: implement the clean crate's batch DerivabilitySolver interface over the ported probe-theory solver (adapter; the solver itself stays ported and headered), and replace the ported deriv_check orchestration + report with deriv_check_clean. Gate: theory suite green + live oracle spot-probes of the Message Derivation Checks wf topic byte-exact (the wellformedness cluster's oracle script can drive the reference binary). When green, DELETE the ported deriv_check.rs.
Where a clean module replaces the last ported holder of a natural name, take the suffix-free name.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).

Reproducing byte-exact HS ids requires 129 that allocation logic, which lives in the ported serializer being "deleted" 130 — writing it into the adapter would transplant replaced logic. 131 132 Consequence: a rewire that kept the ported cell renderer + abbreviation + 133 missing-node fallback + id scheme would GROW `handlers/dot.rs` (adapter on top 134 of a still-live serializer) rather than thin it, and would not be byte-faithful 135 — the opposite of the task's goal. The 221 clean name-only model binds it (`Bound 0`) and reports NO offender — a silent 222 regression. content.text: Let me read the round-4 reports for each cluster to understand what the clean-room closed this round.

content.content:
1	# Round 4 report — wellformedness clean-room (Unit C)
2	
3	Scope: close the two gaps that kept `check_theory` from fully replacing
4	post-elaboration wellformedness checking. All behavior re-derived by probing
5	`oracle/wf_oracle.sh`; no tamarin-rs / Haskell source read. Round-4 probes are in
6	`scratchpad/probes4`, logged in `QUERIES.log`, spec'd in `BEHAVIOR.md`.
7	
8	## GAP 1 — sort-aware quantifier binding
9	
10	The wrong-form-terms check bound quantifiers by NAME only (round-2), which
11	over-binds. The reference distinguishes variable SORTS: a temporal binder `#x`
12	does not bind a message use `x` (`All #x. (A(x) @ #x) ==> (#x = #x)` ->
13	`Formula terms: `Free x'`), and a message binder `x` does not bind a node use
14	`#x` (-> `Free #x`). Fix: the de Bruijn binder stack now carries `(name, sort
15	class)` and matches on both. Sort classes collapse `{Untagged, Msg}` (which is
16	what the round-2 false positive actually needed) but keep Pub/Fresh/Node/Nat
17	distinct. De Bruijn indexing still counts every quantified var, so
18	`All x #x. ... h(x)` -> `h(Bound 1)` (probe g1_dbsort).
19	
20	Probing the sort-annotation interaction surfaced a previously-unimplemented
21	topic, `Quantifier sorts`: quantifying over a public or fresh variable is
22	rejected (`Lemma `L' uses quantifiers with wrong sort: ("x",LSortPub)`); msg,
23	temporal and nat quantifiers are fine. It applies to lemmas and restrictions,
24	lists offending vars as Haskell `(name,LSort)` tuples in binding order with the
25	same width-69 fillSep wrap as Formula terms, and is emitted before Formula terms
26	within an item.
27	
28	## GAP 2 — semantic guardedness
29	
30	Replaced the round-3 heuristic (which recursed the guard through Or/Not/
31	quantifiers) with a decision procedure over the guarded fragment. Guard set of a
32	(sub)formula = the `(name, class)` keys of variables in ACTION atoms reachable
33	through conjunctions only; disjunction, negation, implication, `=`/`<`/`Mset`/
34	subterm/`last` atoms and nested quantifiers contribute no guards (probes
35	gx_disj_ant, gx_neg_ant, gx_less, gx_last, gx_quant_g, gx_eq_ant). A universal is
36	guarded only as `guard ==> rest`; any other body is "universal quantifier without
37	toplevel implication". An existential needs its variables covered by conjunctive
38	action guards, else "unguarded variable(s)". The first failing quantifier is
39	reported (antecedent before consequent, left before right — gr_both, gr_sib);
40	restrictions are lemma-only here (an unguarded restriction is a fatal error).
41	
42	## Assembly and printer
43	
44	- Per-item bundle. Quantifier sorts / Formula terms / Formula guardedness are
45	  emitted item-by-item (all lemmas in source order, then all restrictions), in
46	  that sub-order per item; consecutive same-topic entries merge under one header,
47	  non-consecutive ones become separate blocks (ord_qs_ft_qs -> QS,FT,QS as three
48	  blocks). `Lemma annotations` stays a separate global check after the bundle.
49	  New entry point `checks::formula_reports`.
50	
51	- Col-relative formula printer. Round-3's `base+hang` was off by one at the top
52	  level (gt_and_two_all). The layout engine now hangs at `col + hang` (quantifier
53	  body `col+1`, binary/relational operands `col+0`), and relational atoms break
54	  at their operator with unparenthesised operands (gnest). Narrow formulas are
55	  byte-identical to the single-line printer, so all round-1..3 fixtures are
56	  unchanged.
57	
58	## Tests
59	
60	73 tests pass (was 53). 20 new byte-parity fixtures captured live from the oracle
61	(`tests/fixtures/g4_*.txt`) with hand-built ASTs: sort-aware binding (g1_core /
62	g1_msgnode / g1_dbsort), Quantifier sorts (pub, multi-var wrap, two lemmas,
63	restriction), per-item ordering (ord_qs_ft_qs, ord_la_ft), guardedness decision
64	(universal/existential disjunction, disjunction antecedent, ordering-atom
65	non-guard, partial coverage, recursion order, existential implication, mixed
66	conjunction), and the printer (top-level conjunction break, sibling nesting,
67	relational break + nested hang).
68	
69	## Residual (documented in BEHAVIOR.md)
70	
71	Formula-printer capture-avoidance (`$x.1` when a bound name collides with a free
72	name) is not reproduced; fixtures avoid collisions. Quantifier-head wrapping and
73	fact-argument wrapping are unprobed. Predicate atoms are expanded by the oracle
74	before the check, so they are not expected at the checker; guard extraction
75	treats them as non-guarding defensively.
76

1	# BEHAVIOR.md — Unit D: CLI/console text surface
2	
3	Every statement below traces to an oracle observation captured under
4	`workspace/captures/` (file names in `[brackets]`) or to the SPEC. No
5	tamarin-prover internals were read; all behavior derived from black-box probing
6	of `oracle/hs_oracle.sh`.
7	
8	Oracle note: `hs_oracle.sh <arg1> [rest...]` consumes `arg1` as the first
9	token then appends the rest, i.e. it runs `tamarin-prover <arg1> <rest...>`
10	with stderr merged into stdout. A true zero-argument invocation is therefore
11	NOT observable through this oracle (it always passes at least one token); the
12	closest observable is a single empty-string token (see Ambiguous mode).
13	
14	## 1. Top-level shape
15	
16	The binary is a multi-mode command (cmdargs-style). The first token selects a
17	**mode** by unique prefix match against the mode names; otherwise the default
18	(batch) mode is used and the token is treated as an input FILE.
19	
20	Modes enumerated from help output `[help_global]`:
21	- (default / no token) = batch mode — positional `FILES`.
22	- `interactive` — "Start a web-server to construct proofs interactively." — positional `WORKDIR`.
23	- `variants` — "Compute the variants of the intruder rules for DH-exponentiation." — no positional.
24	- `test` — "Self-test the tamarin-prover installation." — positional `FILES`.
25	
26	Mode prefix matching `[mode_prefix_int/_v/_t]`: `int`→interactive, `v`→variants,
27	`t`→test (any unambiguous non-empty prefix works). An empty token matches all
28	three → ambiguous `[noargs]`.
29	
30	A flag appearing BEFORE a would-be mode token keeps the default batch mode; the
31	token then becomes a filename `[flag_before_mode]`: `-v interactive` →
32	default mode, "interactive" opened as a file → openFile error.
33	
34	## 2. Exit codes
35	
36	- `--help` / per-mode `--help`, `--version`/`-V`, successful batch run: exit 0.
37	- Every parse error and every runtime error observed: exit 1.
38	
39	## 3. `--help` (global and per-mode)  [help_global, help_interactive, help_variants, help_test]
40	
41	Each mode's help has the same skeleton:
42	
43	    <mode-usage-line>
44	      <mode-description>.
45	
46	    Commands:
47	      interactive  Start a web-server to construct proofs interactively.
48	      variants     Compute the variants of the intruder rules for DH-exponentiation.
49	      test         Self-test the tamarin-prover installation.
50	
51	    Flags:
52	      <flag rows, two-column, right column wrapped to a fixed width>
53	    About:
54	      <help/version rows>
55	
56	    <blank>
57	    ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
58	    To show help for different commands, type tamarin-prover [Command] --help.
59	    ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
60	    See 'https://github.com/tamarin-prover/tamarin-prover/blob/master/README.md'
61	    for usage instructions and pointers to examples.
62	    ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
63	    <blank>
64	
65	Usage lines:
66	- global: `tamarin-prover [COMMAND] ... [OPTIONS] FILES`
67	- interactive: `interactive [COMMAND] ... [OPTIONS] WORKDIR`
68	- variants: `variants [COMMAND] ... [OPTIONS]`   (no positional)
69	- test: `test [COMMAND] ... [OPTIONS] FILES`
70	
71	The Commands block is IDENTICAL in all four helps. The footer block (7 lines
72	ending with a trailing blank line) is identical in all four. Every help output
73	ends with `----\n\n` (footer rule + one trailing empty line).
74	
75	The right-hand description column width differs by mode because cmdargs sizes
76	the left column to the widest flag in THAT mode: global uses a very wide left
77	column (widest flag `-m --output-module[=...]`), the other three narrower.
78	Because the exact wrapping/padding is compatibility output, the four help texts
79	are stored verbatim as fixtures and emitted as opaque strings — the clean code
80	does not re-derive column math.
81	
82	Availability of About rows:
83	- default/global mode: `-? --help` AND `-V --version`.
84	- interactive / variants / test: only `-? --help` (no `--version`). Confirmed:
85	  `variants --version` → `Unknown flag: --version` `[version_from_mode]`.
86	
87	### Flag inventory & value semantics (from help text)
88	
89	Value markers: `[=X]` = value OPTIONAL; `=FILE` (no brackets) = value shown
90	required (but see note). Defaults appear in the description text.
91	
92	Default/batch mode flags:
93	- `--prove[=LEMMAPREFIX*|LEMMANAME]`     optional value, repeatable
94	- `--lemma[=LEMMAPREFIX*|LEMMANAME]`     optional value, repeatable
95	- `--stop-on-trace[=DFS|BFS|SEQDFS|SORRY|NONE]`  optional, default DFS
96	- `-b --bound[=INT]`                     optional
97	- `--heuristic[=(C|I|O|P|S|c|i|o|p|s|{.})+]`  optional, default 's'
98	- `--partial-evaluation[=SUMMARY|VERBOSE]`   optional
99	- `-D --defines[=STRING]`                optional
100	- `--diff`                               no value
101	- `--quit-on-warning`                    no value
102	- `--auto-sources`                       no value
103	- `--oraclename[=FILE]`                  optional; default './theory_filename.oracle', fallback './oracle'
104	- `--oracle-only`                        no value
105	- `--quiet`                              no value
106	- `-v --verbose`                         no value
107	- `-c --open-chains[=PositiveInteger]`   optional, default 10
108	- `-s --saturation[=PositiveInteger]`    optional, default 5
109	- `-d --derivcheck-timeout[=INT]`        optional, default 5 (0 deactivates)

111	- `--no-restrictions`                    no value

136	## 4. `--version` and `-V`  [version, short_version]
137	
138	`--version` and `-V` produce identical output (only valid in default mode).
139	The maude readiness check runs concurrently and its lines INTERLEAVE with the
140	version banner in a fixed, reproducible order:
141	
142	    maude tool: '<MAUDE_PATH>'
143	     checking version: <BANNER line 1: tamarin-prover <VER>, (C) <authors>, <YEARS>>
144	    <blank>
145	    This program comes with ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY. It is free software, and you
146	    are welcome to redistribute it according to its LICENSE, see
147	    'https://github.com/tamarin-prover/tamarin-prover/blob/master/LICENSE'.
148	    <blank>
149	    <MAUDE_VER>. OK.
150	     checking installation: OK.
151	    Generated from:
152	    Tamarin version <VER>
153	    Maude version <MAUDE_VER>
154	    Git revision: <GITREV> (with uncommited changes), branch: <BRANCH>
155	    Compiled at: <TIMESTAMP>
156	
157	The banner text (` checking version: ` prefix + tamarin line + warranty block)
158	is emitted between the maude check's "checking version:" and its "<ver>. OK."
159	because the maude subprocess is being awaited. Dynamic/environment slots:
160	`<MAUDE_PATH>` (default 'maude'), `<VER>` (observed 1.13.0), `<YEARS>`
161	(2010-2023), `<MAUDE_VER>` (3.5.1), `<GITREV>` + dirty-suffix + `<BRANCH>`,
162	`<TIMESTAMP>`. These are build/runtime metadata, injected into the template.
163	
164	## 5. maude preamble (framing prelude)
165	
166	Batch/variants runs that CLOSE a theory (need maude) print a 3-line prelude
167	`[batch_nslpk3_default]`:
168	
169	    maude tool: '<MAUDE_PATH>'
170	     checking version: <MAUDE_VER>. OK.
171	     checking installation: OK.
172	
173	`--parse-only` does NOT print the preamble `[batch_nslpk3_parseonly]` (parsing
174	needs no maude). Runtime file-open errors are printed AFTER this preamble.
175	
176	## 6. Batch-mode framing around a processed theory
177	
178	Full default run `[batch_nslpk3_default]`:
179	
180	    <maude preamble (3 lines)>
181	    [Theory <NAME>] Theory loaded
182	    [Theory <NAME>] Theory translated
183	    [Theory <NAME>] Derivation checks started
184	    [Theory <NAME>] Derivation checks ended
185	    [Theory <NAME>] Theory closed
186	    <OPAQUE THEORY PAYLOAD, from `theory <NAME>` through `end`>
187	    <blank>
188	    ==============================================================================
189	    summary of summaries:
190	    <blank>
191	    analyzed: <ABSOLUTE_FILE_PATH>
192	    <blank>
193	      processing time: <T>s
194	      <-- literal 2-space line
195	    <RESULT LINES>
196	    <blank>
197	    ==============================================================================
198	
199	- `====` rule is exactly 78 `=`. Output ends `====\n` (no trailing content).
200	- `--prove` uses the SAME 5 progress phases `[batch_nslpk3_prove]`.
201	- `--parse-only` prints ONLY `[Theory <NAME>] Theory loaded`, then payload; NO
202	  preamble, NO summary block `[batch_nslpk3_parseonly]`.
203	- The "Generated from:" version comment and any `WARNING:` block are part of the
204	  OPAQUE theory payload (inside `theory … end`), not framing.
205	
206	RESULT LINE forms (2-space indent):
207	- `  <lemma> (all-traces): analysis incomplete (<N> steps)`  [default, unselected]
208	- `  <lemma> (all-traces): verified (<N> steps)`             [proved true]  `[batch_nslpk3_prove]`
209	- `  <lemma> (exists-trace): <result> (<N> steps)`           [exists-trace kind]
210	- (form `falsified (<N> steps)` inferred as the counterpart to `verified`; not
211	  directly captured — see LIMITATIONS.)
212	- `  WARNING: <N> wellformedness check failed!`  [when checks failed; note the
213	  singular "check"] `[round2_multiplication_in_rule_lhs]`
214	
215	When a theory has warnings but no lemmas, the WARNING line is the sole result
216	line. Ordering of the WARNING line vs lemma lines when BOTH are present is not
217	observed (LIMITATIONS).
218	
219	`<T>` (processing time) and `<ABSOLUTE_FILE_PATH>` are dynamic slots.

221	## 7. variants mode run  [mode_variants_run]
222	
223	`variants` (no file): maude preamble, then the pretty-printed intruder
224	variant rules (opaque payload), NO summary block.
225	
226	## 8. Error text taxonomy
227	
228	### 8a. cmdargs parse errors — bare one-liner, exit 1, no help:
229	- Unknown long flag `[err_unknown_flag]`:  `Unknown flag: --foobar`
230	- Unknown short flag `[err_unknown_short]`: `Unknown flag: -Z` (note: `-h` is
231	  NOT help; help is `-?`/`--help`) `[short_help]`
232	- Value given to a valueless flag `[help_eq]`: `Unhandled argument to flag, none expected: --help=foo`
233	- Ambiguous mode `[noargs]`: `Ambiguous mode '', could be any of: interactive variants test`
234	
235	### 8b. cmdargs validation errors — message + blank + FULL help of the active mode, exit 1:
236	- No input file (flags otherwise valid) `[err_stopontrace_bad, err_noinput_diff, err_noinput_prove]`:
237	      error: no input files given
238	      <blank>
239	      <global help body>
240	  Envelope = `error: no input files given\n\n` + exact global help.
241	- Bad interactive WORKDIR `[err_two_modes, interactive_two_pos]`:
242	      error: directory '<DIR>' does not exist.
243	      <blank>
244	      <interactive help body>
245	  (`interactive test` → 'test' is the WORKDIR; `interactive aa bb` → names 'bb'.)
246	
247	### 8c. runtime errors — printed AFTER the maude preamble, exit 1:
248	File open (IOException, no CallStack):
249	- missing `[err_missing_file, token_notfile]`:  `tamarin-prover: <path>: openFile: does not exist (No such file or directory)`
250	- unreadable `[err_unreadable]`:                 `tamarin-prover: <path>: openFile: permission denied (Permission denied)`
251	- directory `[err_isdir]`:                       `tamarin-prover: <path>: openFile: inappropriate type (is a directory)`
252	
253	Application `error` with Haskell CallStack (all cite the same call site):
254	- bad bound `[err_bound_nonint]`:
255	      tamarin-prover: bound: invalid bound given
256	      CallStack (from HasCallStack):
257	        error, called at src/Main/Mode/Batch.hs:162:33 in main:Main.Mode.Batch
258	- bad output module `[err_output_module_bad]`:
259	      tamarin-prover: output mode not supported.
260	      CallStack (from HasCallStack):
261	        error, called at src/Main/Mode/Batch.hs:162:33 in main:Main.Mode.Batch
262	
263	These runtime strings (incl. the CallStack source path) are compatibility
264	output taken verbatim from the oracle; they are emitted by the runtime layer
265	after parse(), so the clean crate exposes them as render helpers rather than
266	from parse().
267	
268	## 9. LIMITATIONS / unobserved (documented, not guessed in code)
269	- True zero-argument behavior: not observable through this oracle.
270	- `falsified (<N> steps)` exact string: inferred counterpart of `verified`; not
271	  directly captured (no small example produced it within probe budget).
272	- WARNING-line vs lemma-line ordering in the summary when both present.
273	- Whether `--output-json`/`--output-dot` error at output time when value omitted
274	  (only parse-time / --parse-only observed, where no error occurs).
275	- (RESOLVED in R4) Under `--prove`, extra stderr progress lines DO appear after
276	  `Theory closed` — see §10a.
277	- Interactive-mode value validation (`--port`, `--interface`, `--image-format`):
278	  the server starts even on garbage values (`--port=x .` -> exit 124 server run;
279	  `--image-format=BOGUS .` -> server run), so there is NO CLI-level validation
280	  error to reproduce for those flags `[R4 interactive probes]`.
281	
282	## 10. Stream separation (Round 4)  [split_* captures: <label>.out.txt / .err.txt]
283	
284	The Round 1–3 oracle merged streams (`2>&1`). Re-probing with
285	`1>out 2>err` (probes/split_probe.sh) shows a clean, deterministic split. Each
286	stream is byte-complete on its own; the merged-mode interleaving of §4 was purely
287	a flush artifact of combining two FDs.
288	
289	STDOUT carries:
290	- the opaque theory payload(s) `theory … end` `[split_batch_default.out]`,
291	- the whole "summary of summaries" block `[split_batch_default.out]`,
292	- the `--version`/`-V` banner (tamarin line + warranty + `Generated from:` block)
293	  `[split_version.out — banner ONLY; the merged version.tmpl of §4 wrongly bundled
294	  the maude preamble into stdout]`,
295	- every `--help` / per-mode help page `[split_help.out == help_global.txt]`,
296	- the two cmdargs VALIDATION envelopes `error: … \n\n<full help>`
297	  (no-input, bad-workdir) `[split_err_noinput.out == err_noinput_diff.txt,
298	  split_err_workdir.out == err_two_modes.txt]`.
299	
300	STDERR carries:
301	- the 3-line maude preamble (§5) `[split_batch_default.err]`,
302	- the per-theory progress markers `[Theory <name>] <phase>` `[split_batch_default.err]`,
303	- the bare cmdargs one-liners: `Unknown flag`, `Ambiguous mode`,
304	  `Unhandled argument to flag` `[split_err_unknown.err == err_unknown_flag.txt,
305	  split_err_ambig.err == noargs.txt]`,
306	- all runtime errors: file-open failures and the app `error`/CallStack lines
307	  `[split_err_missing.err, split_err_bound.err]`.
308	
309	`--parse-only` STDERR = one `Theory loaded` line per theory, no preamble; STDOUT =
310	concatenated payloads `[split_batch_parseonly.err/out, split_parseonly_multi]`.
311	`variants` STDOUT = variant payload; STDERR = preamble only `[split_variants.*]`.
312	
313	### 10a. Multi-theory framing  [split_batch_multifile.*]
314	- STDERR: preamble once, then each theory's full 5-phase progress block in order.
315	- STDOUT: payloads concatenated directly (payload_k ends `end\n`, payload_{k+1}
316	  begins `theory …` with NO separator), then ONE combined summary listing every
317	  theory. Payloads are printed only for theories NOT written to an output file.
318	
319	Under `--prove`, a theory's stderr progress block gains extra engine lines AFTER
320	`[Theory <name>] Theory closed`, verbatim `[split_batch_prove.err]`:
321	
322	    [Saturating Sources] Step 1 (Max 5)
323	    [Saturating Sources] Done
324	    [Saturating Sources] Step 1 (Max 5)
325	    [Saturating Sources] Done
326	
327	(`Max 5` = the `--saturation` limit.) A plain analyze run emits none of these. The
328	framing model treats these as an opaque per-theory `extra_progress` slot appended
329	to that theory's five closed phases; the engine supplies the exact text.
330	
331	### 10b. summary-of-summaries exact layout  [split_batch_multifile.out, split_batch_output_single.out]
332	The summary section on STDOUT is:
333	
334	    \n                                  <- separates payload from summary (always emitted)
335	    ==============================================================================   (78 '=')
336	    summary of summaries:
337	    <per theory, each preceded by a blank line:>
338	      analyzed: <PATH-as-passed-on-CLI>
339	      <blank>
340	      [  output:          <OUTPUT-PATH>]   <- only when an output file was written
341	        processing time: <T>s
342	      <2-space line "  ">
343	      <result lines, each "  <text>">
344	    <blank>
345	    ==============================================================================
346	    (final newline)
347	
348	Key/value alignment inside the indented block: labels right-padded to the width of
349	`processing time:` (16), then one space, so values start at column 19 →
350	`  output:` + 10 spaces + value; `  processing time: ` + value. `analyzed:` is a
351	separate un-indented header line with a single space. `output:` is the label for
352	BOTH `-o FILE` and `-O DIR` (the latter writes `<inputstem>_analyzed.spthy`).
353	`analyzed:` echoes the path exactly as given on the command line (relative stays
354	relative) `[R4 relative-path probe]`.
355	
356	## 11. Typed value validation (Round 4)
357	
358	Validation fires ONLY when a value string is present (via `=value`, `--oj=…`, or a
359	short-attached `-b5`); a BARE value flag (`--bound`, `--stop-on-trace`, …) never
360	errors `[R4 bare-vs-valued sweep]`. An empty `=` value IS a present value and is
361	validated (`--bound=` → error, `--stop-on-trace=` → `… method: `). All these errors
362	go to STDERR, exit 1, and use the app `error` shape
363	`tamarin-prover: <msg>\nCallStack (from HasCallStack):\n  error, called at
364	src/Main/Mode/Batch.hs:162:33 in main:Main.Mode.Batch`. In a normal run they follow
365	the maude preamble; under `--parse-only` no preamble precedes them.
366	
367	Integer flags → `<LABEL>: invalid bound given` `[R4 numeric sweep]`:
368	`--bound`→`bound`, `-c/--open-chains`→`OpenChainsLimit`,
369	`-s/--saturation`→`SaturationLimit`, `-d/--derivcheck-timeout`→`derivcheck-timeout`,
370	`--replication-bound`→`replication-bound`.
371	Accepted integer set = Haskell `readMaybe Int`: trims leading/trailing ASCII
372	whitespace, optional `-` (with optional whitespace before the digits), decimal /
373	`0x`hex / `0o`octal, silent overflow wrap; REJECTS `+`, `.`, `_`, trailing
374	non-whitespace, and empty. Negatives and 0 are accepted for ALL of them (the
375	"PositiveInteger" help annotation is not enforced at the CLI).
376	
377	Enum flags:
378	- `--stop-on-trace`: value lowercased, valid ∈ {dfs,bfs,seqdfs,sorry,none};
379	  invalid → `unknown stop-on-trace method: <lowercased-value>` (echoes value).
380	- `--partial-evaluation`: case-insensitive, valid ∈ {summary,verbose};
381	  invalid → `partial-evaluation: unknown option` (no echo).
382	- `--output-module` (`-m`): case-SENSITIVE, valid ∈
383	  {spthytyped,spthy,msr,proverifequiv,proverif,deepsec};
384	  invalid → `output mode not supported.` (no echo).
385	`--heuristic` is NOT validated (any chars accepted).
386	
387	Repeated valued flag → LAST occurrence wins / is the one validated
388	`[R4 repeated probe]`.
389	
390	Multi-invalid precedence is a FIXED order, independent of CLI arg order
391	`[R4 precedence sweep, all pairwise relations consistent]`:
392	`stop-on-trace > bound > partial-evaluation > open-chains > saturation >
393	output-module > derivcheck-timeout > replication-bound`.
394	
395	### 11a. Consumer-extension flags (NOT in the reference, NOT in help)
396	`--processors <positive int>`, `--maude-processes <positive int>`,
397	`--data-dir <path>` all yield `Unknown flag: --<name>` in the reference
398	`[R4 interop probe]`, confirming they are absent. The clean crate accepts them as
399	typed flags with its OWN validation (positive integer; any path); their rejection
400	texts are original (no reference to reproduce) and they are omitted from help.

1	# Unit E — `.spthy` macro expansion — clean-room report
2	
3	Clean-room reimplementation of tamarin-rs macro expansion, derived solely from
4	black-box oracle observation (`oracle/hs_oracle.sh`), the provided example
5	`.spthy` inputs, and the interface AST (`../wellformedness/interface/ast_types.rs`).
6	No tamarin-rs source or tamarin-prover internals were read; every behavioural
7	claim traces to a logged oracle interaction (`QUERIES.log`, tags `[Qn]`).
8	
9	## Deliverables (in `workspace/`)
10	- `macro-clean/` — Rust crate. `pub fn expand(theory: &Theory) -> Theory`
11	  (`src/lib.rs`), AST surface (`src/ast.rs`), tests (`src/tests.rs`).
12	- `BEHAVIOR.md` — the characterised semantics (definition syntax, call-site
13	  expansion, name capture/collision, edge cases, visibility).
14	- `QUERIES.log` — 31 oracle interactions with purpose + observed result.
15	- `byteparity.sh` / `run_tests.sh` — acceptance harness.
16	- `fixtures/` — macro theories paired with hand-inlined equivalents.
17	- `captures/` — raw oracle outputs kept as observation evidence.
18	
19	## What `expand` does
20	Replaces every macro call at every use site with its transitively-substituted
21	body and removes the `macros:` declarations, yielding an equivalent macro-free
22	theory. It performs *only* macro substitution — let-inlining, diff-projection,
23	AC-variant computation and Sapic translation are separate passes and are left
24	intact with their inner macro calls expanded.
25	
26	## Characterised semantics (see BEHAVIOR.md for the full table)
27	- **Definition** `macros: name(f1..fk) = body, ...`; formals untyped; duplicate
28	  formal names rejected [Q22]; a body may reference builtins/functions and
29	  **strictly-earlier macros only** — forward/self/mutual/undeclared references
30	  are parse errors [Q8,Q19,Q20,Q24]. Macros therefore form a DAG and expansion
31	  always terminates. Arity 0 allowed; a body may contain free (non-formal)
32	  variables that are captured by the surrounding scope [Q10].
33	- **Name collisions** rejected at definition time: duplicate macro, or macro
34	  name equal to an actively-declared function/builtin -> `Conflicting name for
35	  macro <n>` [Q13,Q14,Q21]; no conflict when the builtin is absent [Q25].
36	- **Call site** `name(a1..ak)`: bind `fi := ai` and substitute
37	  **simultaneously** (capture-avoiding) [Q7]; a formal matches a body variable
38	  by **full identity including sort** — `~x`/`$x` do not match an untagged
39	  formal `x` and remain free, and an argument whose formal never occurs is
40	  dropped [Q27,Q28]. Expansion is transitive [Q9,Q18].
41	- **Arity** is enforced by the parser: arity >= 2 requires exact argument count
42	  [Q12,Q16,Q17]; for arity 1 extra comma-args are packed into a pair before the
43	  AST [Q11,Q15]. Consequently at the AST level a call's arg-count already equals
44	  the macro arity; `expand` assumes this and no-ops defensively on a mismatch.
45	- **Visibility**: `--parse-only` performs no expansion [Q2]; on close the
46	  `macros:` block and the primary `(modulo E)` rule / lemma / restriction keep
47	  the *call*, while the expansion appears in the `(modulo AC)` variant,
48	  `expanded formula`, `guarded formula`, diff projections, and translated
49	  process rules [Q3,Q4,Q26,Q29]. Macros are not added to the function signature.
50	
51	## Acceptance evidence
52	- **Direct expansion checks** — 14 Rust tests assert `expand()` produces the
53	  exact expected macro-free AST for: simultaneous substitution, nesting, pair
54	  macros, 3-level chains, AST-level pair-packing, sort-sensitive matching,
55	  nullary/free-var bodies, exp+pub literals (both `BinOp` and `AlgApp`),
56	  expansion inside `diff()` and Sapic processes, macro-drop, non-macro-App
57	  preservation, and the defensive arity-mismatch path. All pass.
58	- **Byte-parity fixtures** — for 6 pairs (`capture`, `chain3`, `over_u3`,
59	  `sortmatch`, `issue777`, `MacroInLemmasAndRestrictions`) the oracle output on
60	  the macro theory equals the oracle output on a hand-inlined equivalent once
61	  the three known cosmetic scaffolding categories are removed (preserved
62	  `macros:` block; primary-line call vs expanded term; explicit `(modulo AC)`
63	  block vs trivial-variant note). `byteparity.sh` confirms the remaining
64	  expanded content is byte-identical [Q31]. `run_tests.sh` runs the whole suite:
65	  ALL PASS.
66	
67	## Scope boundaries / notes
68	- The xor example (`MacroWithRestrictionCRxor`) expands structurally to
69	  `h(x XOR y XOR z)` but the oracle then AC-abstracts it into a variant table
70	  (`h(z)`, `z=(...)`); it is therefore excluded from the strict byte-parity set
71	  and only used as a structural observation [Q30].
72	- Macros in `equations:` were not observed in the corpus; `expand` still rewrites
73	  equation terms uniformly (a no-op when none are present) for robustness.
74	- `src/ast.rs` vendors the provided interface type surface so the crate compiles
75	  standalone; at integration the dirty room replaces it with the real type
76	  re-export.
77	
78	## Reproduce
79	```
80	bash workspace/run_tests.sh          # cargo test + all byte-parity fixtures
81	cd workspace/macro-clean && cargo test
82	bash workspace/byteparity.sh <macro.spthy> <hand-inlined.spthy>
83	bash workspace/formula_parity.sh <macro.spthy> <hand-inlined.spthy>  # lemma/acc/case-test
84	```
85	
86	---
87	
88	## Round 4 — three expansion-semantics gaps + declaration preservation
89	
90	Closed three gaps and reconciled the declaration-preservation interop contract.
91	Every claim traces to a round-4 oracle probe ([Q32]–[Q40], `probes/`, `captures/`).
92	
93	**GAP 1 — bare nullary macro uses.** A 0-ary macro used as a plain name (no
94	parentheses) is a use of that macro, treated identically to the parenthesised
95	form: `konst()=h('k')`, bare `konst` → `h('k')` [Q32]. Characterised the exact
96	resolution conditions: it fires in every term position and transitively through
97	bodies [Q32,Q33]; **untagged sort only** (`~konst`/`$konst` stay ordinary vars,
98	and a macro-name use can't be sort-annotated) [Q34,Q32]; **nullary only** (a bare
99	name for an arity ≥ 1 macro stays an ordinary variable) [Q35]; and a nullary
100	macro **reserves its name against a same-named formal** [Q36]. Per the interface
101	contract such a use reaches `expand` as `Term::Var("konst")`; `expand_term` now
102	resolves an untagged `Var` whose name is a nullary macro to that macro's body —
103	and the existing "expand body first, then bind formals" order reproduces the
104	name-reservation result [Q36] by construction.
105	
106	**GAP 2 — accountability-lemma formulas** and **GAP 3 — case-test formulas.**
107	The reference's macro stage DOES expand macros used in `... accounts for "..."`
108	acc-lemma formulas and `test <name>: "..."` case-test formulas. Stage behaviour:
109	`--parse-only` preserves the call; on close the acc lemma is translated into
110	generated lemmas (+`predicate:`) whose primary renderings keep the call and whose
111	`/* guarded formula ... */` blocks show the expansion — the exact
112	primary-keeps-call / guarded-shows-expansion pattern of ordinary lemmas [Q4].
113	Confirmed with identity and non-identity (`h(x)`) macros [Q38,Q39]. `expand`
114	already rewrites `AccLemma.formula` and `CaseTest.formula`; added parity fixtures
115	prove it.
116	
117	**Declaration preservation.** Probed that the reference retains the `macros:`
118	block (with original, un-expanded bodies) in its pretty output after processing
119	[Q37]. Per the interop contract, `expand` now **preserves the `Macros` items in
120	place** (previously it dropped them); only use sites are expanded.
121	
122	**New harness — `formula_parity.sh`.** `byteparity.sh`'s env-stripping approach
123	cannot exercise accountability theories (its reduced env lacks a UTF-8 locale, so
124	the reference aborts on ∀/∃ glyphs before generating lemmas). `formula_parity.sh`
125	runs the oracle with a UTF-8 locale and compares only the guarded/expanded-formula
126	blocks — the harness of record for the lemma/acc-lemma/case-test gaps.
127	
128	**Acceptance (round 4).** `run_tests.sh`: **21** cargo direct-expansion tests
129	(14 prior — one renamed `drops_→preserves_macro_declarations` and one fixture

1	# REPORT — Unit G: Message Derivation Checks (clean-room)
2	
3	## Scope
4	Clean-room reimplementation of the `Message Derivation Checks` wellformedness
5	topic emitted by the reference tamarin-prover binary. Everything below is derived
6	solely from black-box oracle observation (`oracle/hs_oracle.sh`), the captured
7	example outputs, and the SPEC. No file under `/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/` was
8	read; the only oracle interaction was executing the wrapper script (see
9	`workspace/QUERIES.log`). The parsed-theory input model is mirrored from the
10	provided interface header `../wellformedness/interface/ast_types.rs`.
11	
12	## Deliverable
13	Crate `workspace/derivcheck-clean/`:
14	- `src/ast.rs` — mirror of the interface header (type surface only), so the crate
15	  compiles/tests standalone; replaced by the real AST module at integration.
16	- `src/lib.rs` — the check, the callback trait, the decision logic, the renderer,
17	  and 15 unit tests (solver stubbed). `cargo test` -> 15 passed, clean build.
18	
19	## Architecture (callback abstraction)
20	The "can the intruder derive X from Y" decision is the prover's solver and is NOT
21	reimplemented. It is supplied by the caller through:
22	
23	    pub struct DerivProbe<'a> {              // one question per (rule, variable)
24	        pub rule_name: &'a str,
25	        pub rule: &'a Rule,                  // full context (Y = premises, etc.)
26	        pub variable: &'a VarSpec,           // X
27	        pub premises: &'a [Fact],
28	        pub timeout_secs: u64,
29	    }
30	    pub enum Derivability { Derivable, NotDerivable, TimedOut }
31	    pub trait DerivabilitySolver { fn check(&self, probe: &DerivProbe) -> Derivability; }
32	
33	This crate owns everything except the derivability verdict:
34	- Probe construction. `candidate_variables(rule)` = every distinct variable
35	  occurring in the rule's premises/actions/conclusions/let-bindings (a safe
36	  superset; the solver resolves public / `Fr`-bound / state-bound variables to
37	  `Derivable`). Rules are visited in theory order; `IntrRule` items and rules
38	  carrying `[no_derivcheck]` are excluded.
39	- Decision logic. `timeout_secs == 0` deactivates the check (empty report, solver
40	  never consulted). `Derivable` -> skip; `NotDerivable` -> collect; `TimedOut` ->
41	  per `TimeoutPolicy` (default `SuppressRule`).
42	- Presentation. Sort/dedup the flagged variables and render the byte-exact block.
43	
44	Entry points: `message_derivation_checks(thy, solver, timeout_secs)` and the
45	policy-explicit `message_derivation_checks_with(...)`; plus `underline_topic`,
46	`topics`, `render_derivation_report`, `render_variable`, `candidate_variables`,
47	`sort_variables`.
48	
49	## Observed behavior encoded (full detail in BEHAVIOR.md)
50	- Topic string `Message Derivation Checks`, underlined by 25 `=` (= title length),
51	  emitted after `Subterm Convergence Warning` in report order.
52	- Block: header, blank line, a fixed intro paragraph (2-space indent), then one
53	  block per failing rule "Rule <name>: \nFailed to derive Variable(s): <vars>"
54	  (note the trailing space after the colon), blocks separated by blank lines.
55	- Rules in theory (source) order; derivable rules omitted; `[no_derivcheck]`
56	  suppresses a rule.
57	- Reported variables sorted by (sort-rank, name lexicographic, index numeric)
58	  with Fresh < Msg; public names never appear; duplicates collapse. Rendering:
59	  fresh `~name`, public `$name`, message `name`, nonzero index `name.idx`.
60	- `--derivcheck-timeout=INT` (default 5, `=0` deactivates; the `=` is required —
61	  `-d 1` mis-parses `1` as a filename).
62	
63	## Byte-parity evidence
64	The two-rule test string was diffed against the real POIDC_CMB capture:
65	297 bytes each, `cmp` identical. The single-rule and two-rule tests assert the
66	renderer's output equals those byte-exact strings, so the crate reproduces the
67	oracle block byte-for-byte for the observed cases.
68	
69	## Residuals / limits
70	- Per-rule timeout output text is unobserved. The derivation check is robustly
71	  fast (even an 8-term xor rule finishes it quickly; the slowness there is later
72	  precomputation), so a genuine per-rule timeout could not be forced with
73	  practical inputs. The `TimedOut` outcome is modeled and the default policy
74	  (`SuppressRule`, fail-open) follows the only timeout-related observation
75	  (`=0` => no warnings). The alternative (`SkipVariable`) is a single enum arm.
76	- Sort-rank beyond Fresh < Msg and Nat/Node/Pub rendering prefixes were not
77	  exercised because such variables are never reported (always derivable / not
78	  message terms). They are placed last / rendered conservatively, flagged in
79	  comments.
80	- The crate does not emit the driver's phase-log lines
81	  (`[Theory X] Derivation checks started/ended`) or compute the
82	  `WARNING: N wellformedness check failed!` count — those belong to the
83	  wellformedness driver, not this topic.
84	
85	## Integration contract
86	`message_derivation_checks` returns `Vec<WfError>` sharing topic
87	`Message Derivation Checks`: first entry = the intro paragraph, then one entry per
88	failing rule. A report writer renders a topic as
89	`underline_topic(topic) + "\n" + entries.join("\n\n")`, which
90	`render_derivation_report` implements for this topic (verified byte-exact). At
91	integration, thin adapters map these into the shared report and wire the real
92	solver as the `DerivabilitySolver`.
93	
94	---
95	
96	# Round 4 — closing five derivation-check gaps
97	
98	All five gaps were pinned by fresh black-box probes (authored `probes4/*.spthy`, run through
99	`oracle/hs_oracle.sh`; every observation logged in QUERIES.log under `ROUND 4`) and the crate
100	`workspace/derivcheck-clean` was updated to match. `cargo test` → 28 passed, warning-clean; the
101	two-rule block is byte-identical (297 bytes, `cmp`) to the captured reference fixture
102	`probes4/fixture_two_rule.txt` (verified by `examples/dump_two_rule.rs`).
103	
104	## GAP 1 — output contract (one value, heading included)
105	`message_derivation_checks` now returns the WHOLE topic as a SINGLE `WfError` whose `message` is
106	the complete block INCLUDING the underlined `Message Derivation Checks` heading + 25-`=`
107	underline (previously it returned intro + per-rule fragments assembled by a separate renderer).
108	The consuming report renderer adds no per-topic heading; topic blocks are joined by one blank
109	line, which the reference confirms (blank line between the Subterm and Derivation blocks). The
110	returned message is byte-exact and carries no trailing newline. `render_derivation_report` now
111	just returns that single message.
112	
113	## GAP 2 — multi-variable ordering (key was wrong)
114	Discriminating probes (the prior fixtures all used index 0 and could not tell the keys apart)
115	establish the true total order: **`(index numeric, sort-rank Fresh<Msg<Nat, name byte/ASCII)`**
116	— index is PRIMARY, not the sort. Evidence: `In(h(~a.2)),In(h(b))` → `b, ~a.2` (idx-0 msg before
117	idx-2 fresh); `In(h(<z.1,a.2,~z.2,~a.1,m>))` → `m, ~a.1, z.1, ~z.2, a.2`; `In(h(<apple,Zebra>))`
118	→ `Zebra, apple` (ASCII, uppercase first). The old `(sort_rank, name, idx)` key is replaced by
119	`(idx, sort_rank, name)` in `var_order_key`; `sort_rank` gains `Nat=2`. Trailing digits without
120	a `.` stay in the name at index 0 (`x2`,`v10`).
121	
122	## GAP 3 — candidate scope
123	Probed which classes can EVER be flagged. Candidate set = every free variable of the expanded
124	rule across premises, actions AND conclusions: an action-only var (`z`) and a conclusion-only var
125	(`k`) are both flagged. Public (`$p`) is a candidate but the solver always resolves it derivable
126	(never flagged; the unit does not pre-filter). Natural-number vars (`%x`) CAN be flagged and are
127	rendered `%`. Temporal/node vars are a parse error in message-term position — never candidates. A
128	name matching a declared nullary function is a constant (`Term::App(name, [])`), contributing no
129	variable — handled by the term walk. Candidate enumeration was widened to include actions and
130	conclusions accordingly.
131	
132	## GAP 4 — pre-expansion
133	Derivability is decided AFTER macro and `let` expansion; the reported names are the post-expansion
134	inner names (`let y = h(w) in In(y)` → `w`; `let y = w in In(h(y))` → `w`, never `y`;
135	`macros mac(x)=h(x); In(mac(w))` → `w`). The unit now expands the theory-level `macros:` table and
136	the rule's `let` bindings into the rule's fact terms (`expand_rule`, `expand_term`, `subst_vars`,
137	`let_substitution`) before enumerating candidates; let-bound LHS names are eliminated and never
138	become candidates.
139	
140	## GAP 5 — solver interface shape (batched)
141	`DerivabilitySolver` is now batched: `check_rule(&RuleProbe)` receives one rule with ALL its
142	candidate variables and returns one verdict per variable — one saturation per rule, no wasteful
143	per-variable re-saturation. The per-variable path is retained as a thin adapter: `PerVariableSolver`
144	+ `PerVariable<S>` wrapper. A recording-stub test asserts the solver is consulted exactly once per
145	rule with the full candidate batch; fixtures/tests were updated to the batched trait.

//! Integration adapter (workspace-authored): report-order anchor constants for
//! splicing Maude-dependent check results (checkTerms / checkGuarded / SAPIC
//! rule-variants / lhs-rhs) into the wellformedness report at their canonical
//! positions.  The topic strings and their relative order are observed report
//! behavior (see the wf module's canonical order); each `WF_AFTER_*` index is
//! the first entry whose topic sorts after that splicing check.

pub const WF_TOPIC_ORDER: &[&str] = &[
    "Reserved names",
    "Special facts",
    "Fr facts must only use a fresh- or a msg-variable",
    "Fact arity issues",
    "Fact multiplicity issues",
    "Fact capitalization issues",
    "Facts occur in the left-hand-side but not in any right-hand-side ",
    "Unbound variables",
    "Formula terms",
    " Formula guardedness",
    "Lemma annotations",
    "Multiplication restriction of rules",
    "Nat Sorts",
    "Subterm Convergence Warning",
    "Message Derivation Checks",
    "Derivation Checks",
];

pub const WF_AFTER_VARIANTS: usize = 0;
pub const WF_AFTER_FACT_LHS: usize = 8;
pub const WF_AFTER_CHECK_TERMS: usize = 9;
pub const WF_AFTER_CHECK_GUARDED: usize = 10;

1	//! Typed argument parsing with value validation.
2	//!
3	//! [`parse_args`] layers a typed surface over the structural [`crate::parse`]:
4	//! `parse_args(argv) -> Result<Parsed, ParseError>`. On success it yields either a
5	//! help/version request or a fully typed [`Args`] with integers parsed, enums
6	//! resolved, and documented defaults applied. On a value-validation failure it
7	//! yields a [`ParseError`] whose text and stream reproduce the reference binary's
8	//! output byte-for-byte (see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §11).
9	//!
10	//! Only eight flags are value-validated (the rest accept any value). Validation
11	//! fires only for a *present* value (`=value`, `--oj=…`, or short-attached `-b5`);
12	//! a bare flag never fails. Repeated flags follow last-wins. When several flags
13	//! carry an invalid value, a fixed order — independent of command-line order —
14	//! decides which error surfaces:
15	//!
16	//! `stop-on-trace > bound > partial-evaluation > open-chains > saturation >
17	//! output-module > derivcheck-timeout > replication-bound`.
18	
19	use crate::errors::{self, ParseError};
20	use crate::modes::Mode;
21	use crate::parse::{parse, Command, Options};
22	
23	/// The `--stop-on-trace` method (matched case-insensitively).
24	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
25	pub enum StopOnTrace {
26	    Dfs,
27	    Bfs,
28	    SeqDfs,
29	    Sorry,
30	    None,
31	}
32	
33	/// The `--partial-evaluation` mode (matched case-insensitively).
34	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
35	pub enum PartialEval {
36	    Summary,
37	    Verbose,
38	}
39	
40	/// The `-m --output-module` target (matched case-sensitively).
41	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
42	pub enum OutputModule {
43	    SpthyTyped,
44	    Spthy,
45	    Msr,
46	    ProverifEquiv,
47	    Proverif,
48	    Deepsec,
49	}
50	
51	/// Documented default for `--open-chains`.
52	pub const OPEN_CHAINS_DEFAULT: i64 = 10;
53	/// Documented default for `--saturation`.
54	pub const SATURATION_DEFAULT: i64 = 5;
55	/// Documented default for `--derivcheck-timeout`.
56	pub const DERIVCHECK_TIMEOUT_DEFAULT: i64 = 5;
57	
58	/// A fully typed, validated command-line, with defaults applied.
59	///
60	/// Fields cover every flag the mode tables model. Flags irrelevant to the active
61	/// [`Mode`] simply hold their defaults. Bare-vs-valued is preserved only where it
62	/// is semantically load-bearing (the repeatable selectors); other single-valued
63	/// string flags collapse an absent or bare occurrence to `None`.
64	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
65	pub struct Args {
66	    pub mode: Mode,
67	    /// FILES (batch/test) or the WORKDIR token(s) (interactive).
68	    pub positional: Vec<String>,
69	
70	    // -- proving / lemma selection (repeatable; None element = bare occurrence) --
71	    pub prove: Vec<Option<String>>,
72	    pub lemma: Vec<Option<String>>,
73	    pub defines: Vec<Option<String>>,
74	
75	    // -- value-validated flags --
76	    pub stop_on_trace: StopOnTrace,
77	    pub bound: Option<i64>,
78	    pub partial_evaluation: Option<PartialEval>,
79	    pub open_chains: i64,
80	    pub saturation: i64,
81	    pub output_module: Option<OutputModule>,
82	    pub derivcheck_timeout: i64,
83	    pub replication_bound: Option<i64>,
84	
85	    // -- unvalidated value flags (raw last-occurrence value) --
86	    pub heuristic: Option<String>,
87	    pub oraclename: Option<String>,
88	    pub output: Option<String>,
89	    pub output_dir: Option<String>,
90	    pub output_json: Option<String>,
91	    pub output_dot: Option<String>,
92	    pub with_dot: Option<String>,
93	    pub with_json: Option<String>,
94	    pub with_maude: Option<String>,
95	    // interactive-only value flags (unvalidated by the reference)
96	    pub port: Option<String>,
97	    pub interface: Option<String>,
98	    pub image_format: Option<String>,
99	
100	    // -- boolean flags --
101	    pub diff: bool,
102	    pub quit_on_warning: bool,
103	    pub auto_sources: bool,
104	    pub oracle_only: bool,
105	    pub quiet: bool,
106	    pub verbose: bool,
107	    pub no_reuse: bool,
108	    pub no_restrictions: bool,
109	    pub no_compress: bool,
110	    pub parse_only: bool,
111	    pub precompute_only: bool,
112	    pub debug: bool,
113	    pub no_logging: bool,
114	
115	    // -- consumer-extension flags (not in the reference, not in help) --
116	    pub processors: Option<u64>,
117	    pub maude_processes: Option<u64>,
118	    pub data_dir: Option<String>,
119	}

121	/// The outcome of a typed parse: a help/version request, or a run with typed args.
122	///
123	/// `Run` is the common case and the value is consumed immediately, so the size
124	/// gap between variants carries no practical cost.
125	#[allow(clippy::large_enum_variant)]
126	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
127	pub enum Parsed {
128	    Help(Mode),
129	    Version,
130	    Run(Args),
131	}
132	
133	/// Typed parse of an argument vector (excluding argv[0]).
134	pub fn parse_args(argv: &[String]) -> Result<Parsed, ParseError> {
135	    match parse(argv)? {
136	        Command::Help(mode) => Ok(Parsed::Help(mode)),
137	        Command::Version => Ok(Parsed::Version),
138	        Command::Run(spec) => {
139	            let args = build_args(spec.mode, spec.positional, &spec.options)?;
140	            Ok(Parsed::Run(args))
141	        }
142	    }
143	}
144	
145	/// Resolve typed fields, validating values in the fixed precedence order so the
146	/// first invalid flag in that order produces the surfaced error.
147	fn build_args(mode: Mode, positional: Vec<String>, o: &Options) -> Result<Args, ParseError> {
148	    // Precedence order: the earliest invalid flag here is the one that errors.
149	    let stop_on_trace = stop_on_trace_field(o)?;
150	    let bound = int_opt(o, "bound", "bound")?;
151	    let partial_evaluation = partial_eval_field(o)?;
152	    let open_chains = int_default(o, "open-chains", "OpenChainsLimit", OPEN_CHAINS_DEFAULT)?;
153	    let saturation = int_default(o, "saturation", "SaturationLimit", SATURATION_DEFAULT)?;
154	    let output_module = output_module_field(o)?;
155	    let derivcheck_timeout =
156	        int_default(o, "derivcheck-timeout", "derivcheck-timeout", DERIVCHECK_TIMEOUT_DEFAULT)?;
157	    let replication_bound = int_opt(o, "replication-bound", "replication-bound")?;
158	
159	    // Consumer extensions (validated after all reference flags, so a reference
160	    // error always wins).
161	    let processors = positive_int_opt(o, "processors", "--processors")?;
162	    let maude_processes = positive_int_opt(o, "maude-processes", "--maude-processes")?;
163	
164	    Ok(Args {
165	        mode,
166	        positional,
167	        prove: o.occurrences("prove"),
168	        lemma: o.occurrences("lemma"),
169	        defines: o.occurrences("defines"),
170	        stop_on_trace,
171	        bound,
172	        partial_evaluation,
173	        open_chains,
174	        saturation,
175	        output_module,
176	        derivcheck_timeout,
177	        replication_bound,
178	        heuristic: raw_opt(o, "heuristic"),
179	        oraclename: raw_opt(o, "oraclename"),
180	        output: raw_opt(o, "output"),
181	        output_dir: raw_opt(o, "Output"),
182	        output_json: raw_opt(o, "output-json"),
183	        output_dot: raw_opt(o, "output-dot"),
184	        with_dot: raw_opt(o, "with-dot"),
185	        with_json: raw_opt(o, "with-json"),
186	        with_maude: raw_opt(o, "with-maude"),
187	        port: raw_opt(o, "port"),
188	        interface: raw_opt(o, "interface"),
189	        image_format: raw_opt(o, "image-format"),
190	        diff: o.is_set("diff"),
191	        quit_on_warning: o.is_set("quit-on-warning"),
192	        auto_sources: o.is_set("auto-sources"),
193	        oracle_only: o.is_set("oracle-only"),
194	        quiet: o.is_set("quiet"),
195	        verbose: o.is_set("verbose"),
196	        no_reuse: o.is_set("no-reuse"),
197	        no_restrictions: o.is_set("no-restrictions"),
198	        no_compress: o.is_set("no-compress"),
199	        parse_only: o.is_set("parse-only"),
200	        precompute_only: o.is_set("precompute-only"),
201	        debug: o.is_set("debug"),
202	        no_logging: o.is_set("no-logging"),
203	        processors,
204	        maude_processes,
205	        data_dir: raw_opt(o, "data-dir"),
206	    })
207	}
208	
209	/// Last-occurrence raw value (absent or bare → `None`).
210	fn raw_opt(o: &Options, long: &str) -> Option<String> {
211	    match o.last(long) {
212	        Some(Some(v)) => Some(v.to_string()),
213	        _ => None,
214	    }
215	}
216	
217	/// Integer flag with no documented default: `None` when absent/bare, else the
218	/// parsed value or the flag's `invalid bound` error.
219	fn int_opt(o: &Options, long: &str, label: &str) -> Result<Option<i64>, ParseError> {
220	    match o.last(long) {
221	        Some(Some(v)) => match read_haskell_int(v) {
222	            Some(n) => Ok(Some(n)),
223	            None => Err(errors::invalid_bound(label)),
224	        },
225	        _ => Ok(None),
226	    }
227	}
228	
229	/// Integer flag with a documented default applied for absent/bare occurrences.
230	fn int_default(o: &Options, long: &str, label: &str, default: i64) -> Result<i64, ParseError> {
231	    match o.last(long) {
232	        Some(Some(v)) => read_haskell_int(v).ok_or_else(|| errors::invalid_bound(label)),
233	        _ => Ok(default),
234	    }
235	}
236	
237	/// `--stop-on-trace`: case-insensitive; default DFS; invalid value echoes the
238	/// lowercased input.
239	fn stop_on_trace_field(o: &Options) -> Result<StopOnTrace, ParseError> {
240	    match o.last("stop-on-trace") {
241	        Some(Some(v)) => {
242	            let lower = v.to_ascii_lowercase();
243	            match lower.as_str() {
244	                "dfs" => Ok(StopOnTrace::Dfs),
245	                "bfs" => Ok(StopOnTrace::Bfs),
246	                "seqdfs" => Ok(StopOnTrace::SeqDfs),
247	                "sorry" => Ok(StopOnTrace::Sorry),
248	                "none" => Ok(StopOnTrace::None),
249	                _ => Err(errors::unknown_stop_on_trace(&lower)),
250	            }
251	        }
252	        _ => Ok(StopOnTrace::Dfs),
253	    }
254	}
255	
256	/// `--partial-evaluation`: case-insensitive; no default; no value echoed on error.
257	fn partial_eval_field(o: &Options) -> Result<Option<PartialEval>, ParseError> {
258	    match o.last("partial-evaluation") {
259	        Some(Some(v)) => match v.to_ascii_lowercase().as_str() {
260	            "summary" => Ok(Some(PartialEval::Summary)),
261	            "verbose" => Ok(Some(PartialEval::Verbose)),
262	            _ => Err(errors::partial_evaluation_unknown()),
263	        },
264	        _ => Ok(None),
265	    }
266	}
267	
268	/// `-m --output-module`: case-SENSITIVE; no value echoed on error.
269	fn output_module_field(o: &Options) -> Result<Option<OutputModule>, ParseError> {
270	    match o.last("output-module") {
271	        Some(Some(v)) => match v {
272	            "spthytyped" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::SpthyTyped)),
273	            "spthy" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::Spthy)),
274	            "msr" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::Msr)),
275	            "proverifequiv" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::ProverifEquiv)),
276	            "proverif" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::Proverif)),
277	            "deepsec" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::Deepsec)),
278	            _ => Err(errors::output_mode_not_supported()),
279	        },
280	        _ => Ok(None),
281	    }
282	}
283	
284	/// A consumer-extension positive-integer flag: `None` when absent/bare, else a
285	/// value ≥ 1 or an original (non-reference) rejection.
286	fn positive_int_opt(o: &Options, long: &str, flag: &str) -> Result<Option<u64>, ParseError> {
287	    match o.last(long) {
288	        Some(Some(v)) => match v.trim().parse::<u64>() {
289	            Ok(n) if n >= 1 => Ok(Some(n)),
290	            _ => Err(errors::bad_positive_int(flag, v)),
291	        },
292	        _ => Ok(None),
293	    }
294	}
295	
296	/// Parse an integer the way Haskell's `readMaybe :: String -> Maybe Int` does, to
297	/// match the reference's accept/reject boundary (see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §11):
298	/// trims surrounding whitespace, allows a leading `-` with optional whitespace
299	/// before the digits, accepts decimal / `0x`hex / `0o`octal, and rejects `+`, a
300	/// decimal point, underscores, trailing non-whitespace, and the empty string.
301	/// Values beyond the 64-bit range wrap rather than fail, mirroring `fromInteger`.
302	pub fn read_haskell_int(s: &str) -> Option<i64> {
303	    let t = s.trim();
304	    if t.is_empty() {
305	        return None;
306	    }
307	    let (negative, mag) = match t.strip_prefix('-') {
308	        Some(rest) => (true, rest.trim_start()),
309	        None => (false, t),
310	    };
311	    if mag.is_empty() {
312	        return None;
313	    }
314	    let (digits, radix) = if let Some(h) = mag.strip_prefix("0x").or_else(|| mag.strip_prefix("0X"))
315	    {
316	        (h, 16u32)
317	    } else if let Some(oc) = mag.strip_prefix("0o").or_else(|| mag.strip_prefix("0O")) {
318	        (oc, 8u32)
319	    } else {
320	        (mag, 10u32)
321	    };
322	    if digits.is_empty() {
323	        return None;
324	    }
325	    // Accumulate with wrapping so out-of-range inputs mirror Haskell's wrap rather
326	    // than being rejected.
327	    let mut acc: i64 = 0;
328	    for ch in digits.chars() {
329	        let d = ch.to_digit(radix)?;
330	        acc = acc.wrapping_mul(radix as i64).wrapping_add(d as i64);
331	    }
332	    Some(if negative { acc.wrapping_neg() } else { acc })
333	}

fn expand_rule(r: &Rule, table: &MacroTable) -> Rule {
    Rule {
        name: r.name.clone(),
        modulo: r.modulo.clone(),
        attributes: r.attributes.clone(),
        let_block: r.let_block.iter().map(|b| expand_let(b, table)).collect(),
        premises: r.premises.iter().map(|f| expand_fact(f, table)).collect(),
        actions: r.actions.iter().map(|f| expand_fact(f, table)).collect(),
        conclusions: r.conclusions.iter().map(|f| expand_fact(f, table)).collect(),
        embedded_restrictions: r
            .embedded_restrictions
            .iter()
            .map(|p| expand_formula(p, table))
            .collect(),
        variants: r.variants.iter().map(|v| expand_rule(v, table)).collect(),
        left_right: r.left_right.as_ref().map(|(l, rr)| {
            (
                Box::new(expand_rule(l, table)),
                Box::new(expand_rule(rr, table)),
            )
        }),
    }
}

fn expand_sapic_action(a: &SapicAction, table: &MacroTable) -> SapicAction {
    match a {
        SapicAction::New(v) => SapicAction::New(v.clone()),
        SapicAction::Insert(k, v) => {
            SapicAction::Insert(expand_term(k, table), expand_term(v, table))
        }
        SapicAction::Delete(k) => SapicAction::Delete(expand_term(k, table)),
        SapicAction::ChIn { chan, msg } => SapicAction::ChIn {
            chan: chan.as_ref().map(|c| expand_term(c, table)),
            msg: expand_term(msg, table),
        },

            chan: chan.as_ref().map(|c| expand_term(c, table)),
            msg: expand_term(msg, table),

+/// HS `Show LVar`: `sortPrefix s ++ body`, where body is the name (or, if
+/// `idx /= 0`, `name.idx`; if the name is empty, just the index).
+fn show_lvar(v: &p::VarSpec) -> String {
+    let prefix = match v.sort {
+        p::SortHint::Fresh | p::SortHint::Suffix(p::SuffixSort::Fresh) => "~",
+        p::SortHint::Pub | p::SortHint::Suffix(p::SuffixSort::Pub) => "$",
+        p::SortHint::Node | p::SortHint::Suffix(p::SuffixSort::Node) => "#",
+        p::SortHint::Nat | p::SortHint::Suffix(p::SuffixSort::Nat) => "%",
+        _ => "",
+    };
+    let body = if v.name.is_empty() {
+        v.idx.to_string()
+    } else if v.idx == 0 {
+        v.name.clone()
+    } else {
+        format!("{}.{}", v.name, v.idx)
+    };
+    format!("{}{}", prefix, body)
+}
